title: Carved oak basting spoon
subtitle: A long-handled seasoned-oak spoon for basting, shaped with gouges and sloyd knife
excerpt: A basting spoon needs a long handle, a rounded bowl with high walls to hold liquids, and a hard close-grained wood that resists the repeated heating of a roasting pan. Seasoned oak provides all three. This intermediate project covers gouge-hollowing the bowl, sloyd-knife handle shaping, and finishing with boiled linseed oil.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the gouge-and-knife method on seasoned hardwoods in domestic utensil work.

glossaryTerms.definition: Timber air-dried or kiln-dried to a stable moisture content. Seasoned oak is dense, close-grained, and highly heat-resistant.
The curvature of a gouge cutting edge, numbered 1 (almost flat) to 11 (deep U-profile). A sweep-7 has a moderately curved edge; it is the standard bowl-roughing gouge.
The sawn piece of timber from which a finished utensil is carved.

content.text: A basting spoon works harder than a dessert spoon: the bowl fills with hot fat, the long handle keeps the hand clear of a deep roasting pan, and the piece may sit in an oven at 200°C for minutes at a time.
Seasoned oak

is the right material. Its density resists the thermal stress, its tight grain does not absorb fat deeply, and a well-made oak basting spoon will outlast several generations of use. Beech is a close second; ash is lighter but still adequate. This project uses a
sweep-7 gouge
for the bowl hollow rather than a hook knife, because seasoned oak is too hard for comfortable hook-knife hollowing over the depth required.
What you need: wood
A sawn
blank

of seasoned oak or beech: 38 cm long, 6 cm wide, 18 mm thick. The grain must run along the blank's length. A quartersawn blank (growth rings running roughly perpendicular to the wide face) is more stable than a flatsawn one; the bowl walls will be less likely to distort with seasonal movement. Moisture content should be at or below 12 per cent.
What you need: tools

A sloyd knife for the profile and handle. A 12 mm sweep-7 gouge to rough out the bowl hollow. A medium hook knife to refine the bowl walls. A wooden or carpenter's mallet to drive the gouge on the hard seasoned oak. A leather strop for both knives; an India oilstone for the gouge. Sandpaper at 120 grit then 180 grit. Boiled linseed oil for the finish; note that boiled linseed oil is not food-safe and should be used only on basting spoons that will not contact food directly. If direct food contact is intended, use raw linseed oil instead and cure for three to four weeks.
Safety

Hold the blank in a vice or between bench dogs for gouge work: a gouge driven with a mallet into unsecured wood can kick. Keep the free hand behind the cutting edge at all times. Oil-soaked rags with boiled linseed oil must be spread flat to dry or stored in water before disposal; they can heat spontaneously.
The work
Step 1: Saw or knife the plan profile

Mark the plan profile on the blank. The bowl is 10 cm long and 7 cm wide, with walls rising 12 mm above the base of the hollow. The handle is 24 cm long, tapering from 40 mm at the shoulder to 22 mm at the tip. A tenon saw removes the corners quickly; a sloyd knife finishes the outline with push cuts along the pencilled line.
Step 2: Gouge out the bowl hollow

Clamp the blank in a vice with the bowl face up. Place the sweep-7 gouge bevel-down across the bowl area and drive it with light mallet blows, working from the centre of the bowl outward in a series of overlapping passes. The gouge removes material in curved chips; the depth target is 10 to 12 mm at the bowl centre. Work with the grain from the rim inward: cuts that go against the grain will tear the oak surface.
Step 3: Refine the bowl with the hook knife

The gouge leaves a faceted surface. A medium hook knife, used with short controlled pull strokes from the chest-brace position, refines the bowl to a smooth concave. Aim for even wall thickness of 5 to 6 mm for a basting spoon (thicker than an eating spoon, because the bowl must hold hot liquid without flexing). Check by feeling the back of the bowl for ridges while running the hook knife inside.
Step 4: Shape the handle

The basting spoon handle is oval in section, 20 mm by 14 mm at the grip. Take push cuts along the handle edges with the sloyd knife to reduce the rectangular section to an oval. A slight widening at the end of the handle (a terminal swell of 25 mm) keeps the spoon from slipping through a wet grip. Refine the shoulder taper with short thumb-pivot cuts.
Step 5: Sand
Sand the bowl interior at 120 grit, then 180 grit. Sand the handle at 180 grit only; the rougher knife finish on the handle provides grip. Brush off all dust before oiling.
Finishing

Apply boiled linseed oil generously to the whole spoon. Allow to penetrate for thirty minutes, then wipe off all surface oil. Boiled linseed oil sets within 24 hours. Apply two coats 24 hours apart. The finished spoon resists the heat and fat of basting well. Boiled linseed oil contains metal driers and is not considered food-safe; for direct food contact, use raw linseed oil with a three-to-four-week cure instead.
Variations

Beech is easier to work than oak and produces a very similar result. The grain is more uniform; the surface is slightly lighter in colour. Beech is the kitchen-treen standard in northern European domestic tradition.
A shorter version (28 cm total, 8 cm bowl) serves as a large serving spoon for thick stews and soups, where the deep bowl holds more than a shallow dessert-spoon form.
Care
Wash in warm soapy water, dry at once. Re-oil with a wipe of boiled linseed once a year or when the surface looks dull.

items.symptom: The gouge tears the surface rather than cutting cleanly
items.cause: The cut is going against the grain direction, or the gouge edge is dull.
items.fix: Reverse the cut direction. For oak, the gouge must be sharper than for softer woods; hone on the India oilstone before the session and strop every ten minutes.

items.symptom: The bowl walls are uneven in thickness
items.cause: The gouge passes overlapped unevenly, removing more material on one pass than the adjacent one.
items.fix: Use a flat-ended pencil to mark a depth target at the bowl centre; take passes from the rim to the target line rather than freehand across the bowl.

items.symptom: The handle cracks along the grain after finishing
items.cause: The oak had a higher moisture content than 12 per cent when worked. As it dried further, the handle split.
items.fix: Always check seasoned timber with a moisture meter before fine work. Hairline splits along the grain can be filled with a wood-glue-and-sawdust paste and refinished.
